data.title: Is Evercore (EVR) Pricing Reflect Recent Dealmaking Headlines Or Offer Long Term Value?
data.description:

  • Recent news coverage has focused on Evercore's role as an independent investment banking adviser and its position within global deal making. This helps frame why investors react strongly to changes in activity across mergers, acquisitions and capital markets. Investors have also been watching how advisory focused firms like Evercore respond to shifts in transaction pipelines and corporate confidence, which often feeds directly into share price moves.
  • On Simply Wall St's valuation checks, Evercore scores 3 out of 6, giving it a valuation score of 3. Next we will look at what that means using common valuation approaches before considering a more complete way to think about value at the end of the article.

Approach 1: Evercore Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to generate above the return required by its shareholders, then capitalises those extra earnings into an intrinsic value per share.

For Evercore, the model uses a Book Value of $46.93 per share and a Stable EPS of $28.72 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 4 analysts. The Cost of Equity is set at $5.47 per share, which implies an Excess Return of $23.25 per share. In other words, the analysis assumes Evercore may earn more on its equity base than the return investors require.

The Average Return on Equity feeding into this is 42.14%, alongside a Stable Book Value of $68.15 per share, sourced from weighted future Book Value estimates from 3 analysts. Taken together, these inputs feed into an intrinsic value estimate of about $571.02 per share using the Excess Returns framework.

Against the recent share price of $331.04, this Excess Returns valuation implies the stock is about 42.0% undervalued on these assumptions.

Approach 2: Evercore Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Evercore, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay for each share to the earnings the business is currently generating. It helps you see how many dollars investors are willing to pay today for one dollar of annual earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk tends to line up with a lower, more conservative multiple.

Evercore’s current P/E is 21.63x. That sits below the Capital Markets industry average of 23.23x, but above the peer group average of 18.24x. data.title: Occidental Petroleum Debt Moves And Dividend Shift Reframe Investor Tradeoffs
data.description:
• Occidental Petroleum (NYSE:OXY) has launched a series of cash tender offers for certain senior notes and debentures.
  • The company is using proceeds from the completed US$9.7b OxyChem divestiture to reduce debt.
  • Alongside the liability actions, Occidental has announced an 8% quarterly dividend increase.

Occidental Petroleum, a large upstream and integrated energy company, is in a new phase of balance sheet repair following its Anadarko acquisition period. The combination of debt tenders and a higher dividend puts capital structure decisions back in focus for investors who follow NYSE:OXY. These moves arrive as energy companies continue to weigh cash returns to shareholders against long term spending needs.

For you as an investor, the key questions now center on how much interest expense Occidental may retire, how flexible its balance sheet becomes, and what that could mean for future capital allocation choices. The latest actions provide fresh information on management priorities, which you can weigh alongside your own view of the energy sector and your risk tolerance.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Occidental Petroleum by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Occidental Petroleum.

Is Occidental Petroleum's balance sheet strong enough for future acquisitions? Dive into our detailed financial health analysis.

Occidental Petroleum is using the US$9.7b OxyChem proceeds to go straight at some of its higher coupon and longer-dated obligations, which can matter a lot for long term interest costs. The tender offers cover several series of senior notes and debentures, including zero coupon notes due 2036 and multiple series with coupons between 6.125% and 7.950% that run through 2031. By targeting these instruments, Occidental is trying to reduce its principal debt load and potentially lower average funding costs, which can free up more operating cash for other uses over time.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Occidental Petroleum Narrative

  • The tender offers and earlier US$5.8b of debt reduction since mid December are consistent with the focus on deleveraging that features in the existing Occidental narrative, and support the idea of using cash flows and divestiture proceeds to strengthen the balance sheet.
  • At the same time, the decision to increase the quarterly dividend by more than 8% introduces a pull in the other direction, as it commits more cash to shareholder payouts that might otherwise have been used for faster debt reduction or for projects like carbon capture.
  • The consent solicitations tied to several of these notes and the sub cap on the zero coupon 2036 notes add detail on liability management that is not fully reflected in the high level narrative about deleveraging and may change how flexible the capital structure becomes.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Occidental Petroleum to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged a high level of debt, so even with these tenders, interest obligations and refinancing needs remain a key consideration if oil prices stay under pressure.
  • ⚠️ Large one off items linked to the OxyChem divestiture have already affected results, and further balance sheet moves could add more complexity to reported earnings.
  • 🎁 Earnings are forecast to grow, and reducing higher coupon notes can support that by lowering future interest expense and increasing financial flexibility.
  • 🎁 A higher dividend and continued deleveraging both point to management balancing cash returns with balance sheet repair, which some investors may view as a more shareholder friendly stance.

What To Watch Going Forward

You will want to track how much of each note series Occidental actually repurchases relative to the amounts outstanding, and how that translates into changes in total debt and interest expense. The early tender premium of US$30 per US$1,000 of principal and the different priority levels for each security will influence that outcome. It is also worth following how rating agencies and analysts respond to the mix of debt reduction and dividend growth, and how Occidental’s approach compares with other integrated and upstream names such as Chevron, ExxonMobil and ConocoPhillips. Those peers provide context for how much flexibility Occidental has if oil prices or cash flows change.

data.description:

  • The current focus is on moving from approval to broader real-world deployment across eligible patient populations.

For you as an investor, Casgevy sits at the intersection of Vertex's established role in serious diseases and the growing use of gene-editing tools in medicine. While Vertex is already known for its work in other therapeutic areas, Casgevy adds exposure to a treatment approach that targets the underlying genetic drivers of disease rather than only managing symptoms.

The scale and pace of Casgevy uptake could influence how investors think about NasdaqGS:VRTX and gene-editing companies more broadly over time. As more patients start treatment, the focus is likely to stay on how smoothly the real-world rollout proceeds, patient access, and how payers respond to this kind of high-complexity therapy.

For Vertex, scaling up Casgevy treatments with CRISPR Therapeutics looks like a concrete step in shifting from a cystic fibrosis focused story to one where gene-editing and non CF products play a bigger role. Management is already pointing to at least US$500 million in 2026 revenue from non CF products, with Casgevy specifically called out alongside JOURNAVX. That sits next to full year 2025 revenue of US$12.0b and a move from a US$535.6 million net loss to US$3.95b in net income, which shows the company now has meaningful earnings support for these newer launches.

How This Fits Into The Vertex Pharmaceuticals Narrative

  • Casgevy uptake adds weight to the narrative that Vertex can use its cystic fibrosis cash flows to build a broader precision medicine portfolio, including gene-editing for blood disorders.
  • If real world adoption of Casgevy or reimbursement terms are slower than Vertex anticipates, that could challenge expectations that new launches will materially expand earnings beyond the core cystic fibrosis franchise.
  • The specific revenue contribution from Casgevy within the US$500 million non CF guidance, and how that compares with ALYFTREK and JOURNAVX, is not fully detailed in the narrative and may become more important as reported figures break this out.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Vertex Pharmaceuticals to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Execution risk as Vertex and CRISPR Therapeutics move Casgevy from initial approvals into wider commercial use, including complex manufacturing and treatment center coordination.
  • ⚠️ Uncertainty around long term payer acceptance and pricing for high complexity gene-editing therapies, which could limit how large the addressable revenue pool becomes.
  • 🎁 Casgevy gives Vertex exposure to gene-editing alongside larger peers such as Pfizer and Regeneron that are also active in genetic medicines, potentially broadening future revenue beyond cystic fibrosis.
  • 🎁 Management is guiding to US$12.95b to US$13.1b of 2026 revenue with at least US$500 million from non CF products, showing Casgevy is already part of a more diversified earnings mix.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it makes sense to watch how quickly Vertex ramps patient infusions through its authorized treatment center network, how much of the non CF revenue bucket Casgevy represents as quarterly disclosures build up, and whether real world outcomes support broader use. You can also pay attention to how competitors in genetic medicine, such as CRISPR-focused peers and larger biopharma companies, respond with their own treatments and pricing. data.title: JLL (JLL) Net Margin Improvement Challenges Longstanding Bearish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Jones Lang LaSalle (JLL) has put up a busy FY 2025 so far, with Q3 revenue at US$6.5b and basic EPS of US$4.71, alongside trailing 12 month revenue of US$25.3b and EPS of US$13.31 that captures the broader earnings cycle. Over recent quarters the company has seen revenue move from US$5.7b and EPS of US$1.17 in Q1 2025 to US$6.3b and EPS of US$2.37 in Q2, and then to US$6.5b and EPS of US$4.71 in Q3, with a 32.1% earnings increase over the past year setting the backdrop for a business where margins have been edging higher and now sit at the center of the story for investors.

See our full analysis for Jones Lang LaSalle.

With the latest quarter on the table, the next step is to see how these margin trends and earnings shifts line up against the dominant narratives around JLL’s growth potential and risk profile.

See what the community is saying about Jones Lang LaSalle

Advertisement

Net margin edges up to 2.5%

  • Over the last 12 months, JLL generated US$25.3b of revenue and US$631.6 million of net income, which works out to a 2.5% net margin compared with 2.1% a year earlier.
  • Analysts with a bullish view argue that investments in technology and a unified global platform can keep lifting margins. However, the current 2.5% net margin and five year earnings decline of about 10.7% per year show that recent progress is still coming off a relatively low profitability base.
    • The bullish case leans on margins rising from 2.3% to 3.5% over three years, while the historical 10.7% yearly earnings decline highlights how dependent that view is on a sustained shift in the business mix and cost structure.
    • With trailing 12 month EPS at US$13.31, bulls see room for EPS to grow meaningfully from here, but the margin history makes it important to check whether high margin, recurring services are actually becoming a larger share of that US$25.3b revenue pool.

Strong margin talk is easy, but the numbers are what matter, and bulls are hanging their case on that 2.5% net margin turning into something much more durable over time. 🐂 Jones Lang LaSalle Bull Case

TTM earnings up 32.1% despite weak 5 year trend

  • Reported earnings over the trailing 12 months grew 32.1%, even though the longer five year trend shows annual earnings declining about 10.7% per year.
  • Bears focus on that 10.7% yearly earnings decline and structural risks to leasing and capital markets, and they view the 32.1% one year earnings jump as potentially fragile given how exposed JLL is to office demand, interest rates, and transaction volumes.
    • The cautious view points to the reliance on transaction based fees, so a single strong year does not yet address concerns about how the business might perform if leasing and deal activity slow again.
    • At the same time, the recent EPS progression within FY 2025, from US$1.17 in Q1 to US$2.37 in Q2 and US$4.71 in Q3, shows momentum that bears need to weigh against their thesis of slower, more volatile earnings over time.

That tension between a 32.1% one year earnings jump and a weaker five year trend is exactly what skeptics are watching to judge how repeatable this year really is. 🐻 Jones Lang LaSalle Bear Case

P/E of 23.5x and 33.5% DCF discount

  • JLL trades on a trailing P/E of 23.5x at a share price of US$314.42, below both the peer average of 29.7x and the US real estate industry at 27.3x, and about 33.5% below an indicated DCF fair value of roughly US$472.79.
  • Consensus narrative points to moderate forecast earnings growth of about 9.1% per year and revenue growth of around 5.4% per year, both below the referenced US market forecasts. As a result, the relatively lower P/E and large gap to the US$472.79 DCF fair value are being weighed against expectations for only mid single digit to high single digit growth.
    • Analysts referencing this view see JLL as cheaper than peers on P/E, yet they are not projecting high growth, which helps explain why the shares do not trade closer to that DCF fair value.
    • The combination of a 2.5% net margin, 23.5x P/E, and earnings forecast to grow about 9.1% per year leaves investors to decide whether the discount to peers and to the DCF value fairly reflects the growth and profitability profile described in the consensus narrative.
